tiers.name: main entrypoints
tiers.description: Primary SDK surface. 80% of users interact only at this tier.

interfaces.name: run_cycle
interfaces.signature: run_cycle(snapshot: dict[str, Any], *, persistence: WorkspacePersistence | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]
interfaces.intent: Drive one full orchestration cycle: workset selection, concept collection, planning, batch dispatch, verification, and requirement patching. Returns a structured result dict with cycle_id, dispatches, results, events, and updated snapshot.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Clear single-responsibility. Optional persistence kwarg follows Pit of Success pattern: zero-config works, persistence is opt-in. Return type as dict[str,Any] trades IDE discoverability for flexibility — a typed CycleResult dataclass would improve ergonomics but is not essential for v1.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.graph.builder import run_cycle
result = run_cycle(snapshot)
print(result['cycle_id'], result['dispatches'])

interfaces.name: run_fixture_cycle
interfaces.intent: Load a built-in fixture snapshot (game_project, ecommerce_project) and run one minimal orchestration cycle. Primary 5-minute evaluation path.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Minimal surface. String fixture name is discoverable via CLI help. Auto-applies sibling project_config.json for zero-config fixture runs.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.main import run_fixture_cycle
result = run_fixture_cycle('game_project')
print(result['cycle_id'])

interfaces.name: run_snapshot_cycle
interfaces.signature: run_snapshot_cycle(snapshot_path: str | Path, *, project_config_path: str | Path | None = None, persistence_root: str | Path | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]
interfaces.intent: Run one orchestration cycle from an arbitrary snapshot file. Primary programmatic entry for CI/scripted integration.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Accepts str or Path per Python conventions. All optional params are keyword-only, preventing positional mistakes. Persistence is opt-in.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.main import run_snapshot_cycle
result = run_snapshot_cycle('.devforge/devforge.snapshot.json',
    project_config_path='.devforge/devforge.project_config.json',
    persistence_root='.devforge')

interfaces.name: main
interfaces.intent: CLI entrypoint dispatching to init / snapshot / fixture / doctor / repl subcommands. Usable as a library function for subprocess-free testing.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Optional argv kwarg allows test-time injection without subprocess overhead. Returns integer exit code per POSIX convention.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.main import main
exit_code = main(['fixture', 'game_project'])

interfaces.name: run_executor_doctor
interfaces.signature: run_executor_doctor(*, cwd: str | Path = '.') -> dict[str, Any]
interfaces.intent: Probe local executor readiness (codex, claude, python) outside the orchestration graph. Returns per-executor availability, status, and actionable summary.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Pure diagnostic function with no side effects. Returns structured dict so callers can parse programmatically. Actionable error messages (e.g., 'claude is installed but not logged in for non-interactive use') are consistent with Midori error model.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.main import run_executor_doctor
result = run_executor_doctor(cwd='/my/project')
for check in result['checks']:
    print(check['executor'], check['status'])

tiers.name: extension interfaces
tiers.description: Adapter implementer surface. Used when adding new executor backends or tuning dispatch behavior.

interfaces.name: ExecutorAdapter
interfaces.signature: Protocol with: name: str, supports_phase(phase) -> bool, supports_role(role_id) -> bool, estimate(wp) -> dict, prepare_request(wp, ctx) -> dict, submit(request) -> SubmissionReceipt, submit_request(request, *, accepted) -> ExecutorDispatch, dispatch(wp, ctx) -> ExecutorDispatch, default_pull_strategy(wp, ctx) -> dict, pull_context(refs, *, broker, mode, budget) -> list[ResolvedContext], poll(execution_id) -> dict, cancel(execution_id) -> dict, normalize_result(raw) -> ExecutorResult
interfaces.intent: Structural Protocol defining the pluggable executor backend contract. Implement all 11 methods and register to replace or extend executor routing.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Protocol-based typing is Pythonic and avoids ABC overhead. 11 methods is substantial — BaseExecutorAdapter provides sensible defaults for ~8 of them, reducing required implementation to 3 core methods (submit, normalize_result, plus CLI-specific helpers). submit vs submit_request vs dispatch naming requires careful reading of docs to understand the progression.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.executors.base import ExecutorAdapter, SubmissionReceipt, ExecutorDispatch
from devforge.executors import register_executor_adapter

class MyAdapter:
    name = 'my_tool'
    def supports_phase(self, phase): return True
    def dispatch(self, wp, ctx): ...
    # ... implement remaining methods

register_executor_adapter(MyAdapter())

interfaces.name: BaseExecutorAdapter
interfaces.signature: BaseExecutorAdapter(name: str, supported_phases: tuple[str, ...], supported_roles: tuple[str, ...])
interfaces.intent: Concrete base class implementing all ExecutorAdapter methods with stub/SubprocessTransport routing. Subclass and override submit() or build_request() to implement a new executor.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Dataclass with slots for efficiency. SubprocessTransport lifecycle (submit/poll/cancel) is fully handled, reducing custom adapter to CLI argv construction only.

interfaces.name: ExecutorDispatch
interfaces.signature: ExecutorDispatch(execution_id: str, executor: str, work_package_id: str, accepted: bool, message: str = '', metadata: dict = {})
interfaces.intent: Normalized dispatch record returned immediately after task submission, carrying execution tracking metadata.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Dataclass with slots. All fields are named clearly. accepted: bool is the primary branch signal.

interfaces.name: register_executor_adapter
interfaces.intent: Register a custom adapter in the global EXECUTOR_REGISTRY, making it available for phase/role routing.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Simple registry function. EXECUTOR_REGISTRY is a dict keyed by adapter.name — predictable lookup. Import side effects (registry population at __init__.py import time) follow common Python convention.

interfaces.name: SnapshotStore
interfaces.signature: Protocol: load_snapshot(name) -> dict, save_snapshot(name, data) -> None, list_snapshots() -> list[str]
interfaces.intent: Structural Protocol for snapshot persistence backends. Implement to swap in a custom storage layer (e.g., S3, Postgres).
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Minimal 3-method Protocol. load/save/list naming is consistent and conventional.
interfaces.example:
class S3SnapshotStore:
    def load_snapshot(self, name): ...
    def save_snapshot(self, name, data): ...
    def list_snapshots(self): ...

interfaces.name: build_local_workspace_persistence
interfaces.intent: Factory that assembles the four-layer persistence stack (SQLite snapshots + events, JSON memory, filesystem artifacts) under the given root directory.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Single-call factory following Pit of Success pattern. Caller does not need to know internal store types.

tiers.name: config and state interfaces
tiers.description: Advanced configuration and domain model surface. Used for fine-grained control and graph contribution.

interfaces.name: load_project_config / apply_project_config
interfaces.signature:
load_project_config(path: str | Path) -> dict[str, Any]
apply_project_config(snapshot: dict, config: dict) -> dict[str, Any]
interfaces.intent: Load a devforge.project_config.json file and overlay its llm_preferences, knowledge_preferences, and pull_policy_overrides onto a snapshot dict before cycle execution.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Clean separation of load vs apply enables testing apply without filesystem. Returns a new snapshot copy — no mutation.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.config import load_project_config, apply_project_config
config = load_project_config('.devforge/devforge.project_config.json')
snapshot = apply_project_config(snapshot, config)

interfaces.name: WorkPackage
interfaces.signature: WorkPackage(work_package_id, initiative_id, project_id, phase, domain, role_id, title, goal, status: WorkPackageStatus, executor, ...)
interfaces.intent: Core domain model for a bounded execution unit. 9-status lifecycle (proposed→ready→running→blocked/waiting_review→completed/verified/failed/deprecated).
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Immutable dataclass with clear field names. 30+ fields creates a high construction cost for tests — fixture JSON files are the primary construction path. Field naming is mostly consistent with one exception: work_package_id vs project_id vs initiative_id use different abbreviation levels.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.state import decode_snapshot
data = decode_snapshot(snapshot)
for wp in data['work_packages']:
    print(wp.work_package_id, wp.status)

interfaces.name: decode_snapshot / encode_snapshot
interfaces.signature:
decode_snapshot(raw: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]
encode_snapshot(typed: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]
interfaces.intent: Convert raw JSON snapshot dicts to/from typed domain model dataclasses. The single serialization boundary for all snapshot I/O.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Returns dict[str, Any] rather than a named container, which means callers must know the key names ('work_packages', 'projects', etc.) by convention. A SnapshotContents named tuple or dataclass would improve type safety.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.state import decode_snapshot
types = decode_snapshot(raw_dict)
work_packages = types['work_packages']  # list[WorkPackage]

interfaces.name: build_llm_client_from_config
interfaces.intent: Factory that instantiates the correct LLM provider client (OpenRouter, GoogleGenAI, Mock) from a preferences dict.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Factory pattern is standard. Config dict schema mirrors llm_preferences from project_config — consistent vocabulary.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.llm import build_llm_client_from_config
client = build_llm_client_from_config({'provider': 'openrouter', 'model': 'claude-sonnet-4-5'})

interfaces.name: RuntimeState
interfaces.signature: RuntimeState(workspace_id: str, initiative_id: str | None = None, foreground_project: str | None = None, ...)
interfaces.intent: Mutable operational dataclass passed between all graph nodes in a single cycle. Carries cycle tracking, workset selection, knowledge, dispatch results, and termination signal.
interfaces.ergonomics_notes: Typed dataclass with explicit fields improves IDE discoverability over dict mutation. Some field names are cycle-internal (e.g., replan_reason, termination_signal) and should not be set by external callers — documentation boundary needs clarification.
interfaces.example:
from devforge.graph.runtime_state import RuntimeState
state = RuntimeState(workspace_id='ws-1', initiative_id='i-1')
print(state.termination_signal, state.current_workset)

overall_philosophy: Python owns orchestration, executor owns bounded work. Explicit Protocol over convention, typed RuntimeState over dynamic dicts. Progressive Disclosure: Tier 1 requires zero configuration; Tier 2 requires only Protocol implementation; Tier 3 exposes full internals for graph contributors.
consistency_issues: submit vs submit_request vs dispatch method chain on ExecutorAdapter is confusing — three methods with overlapping responsibilities and unclear call sequence
run_cycle returns dict[str,Any] while the node spec recommends a typed CycleResult — return type opaqueness forces callers to read tests to learn key names
decode_snapshot returns dict[str,Any] with string keys — no SnapshotContents typed container means callers must know 'work_packages', 'projects', 'seams' by convention
stubs.py exports production adapters (not test stubs) — naming is misleading and conflicts with file-catalog.json description (C004)
WorkPackage has 30+ fields but no builder or factory helper — test construction via raw dict is verbose and error-prone
PLAN.md phase 1/2 descriptions are out of date with implementation (C002, C003) — new contributors may have wrong expectations about project maturity
